title: Heidelberg, Germany

infobox.value: ([/ˈhaɪdəlbɜːrɡ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[3]](#cite_note-3) German: [[ˈhaɪdl̩bɛʁk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Heidelberg-Aussprache.ogg "File:Heidelberg-Aussprache.ogg"); [Palatine German](/wiki/Palatine_German_language "Palatine German language"): *Heidlberg*) is the [fifth-largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg_by_population "List of cities in Baden-Württemberg by population") in the [German state](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") of [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg"), and with a population of about 163,000, of which roughly a quarter consists of students,[[4]](#cite_note-4) it is [Germany's 51st-largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Germany_by_population "List of cities in Germany by population"). Located about 78 km (48 mi) south of [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), Heidelberg is part of the densely populated [Rhine-Neckar Metropolitan Region](/wiki/Rhine-Neckar "Rhine-Neckar") which has its centre in [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim").

**Heidelberg** ([/ˈhaɪdəlbɜːrɡ/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English");[[3]](#cite_note-3) German: [[ˈhaɪdl̩bɛʁk]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Heidelberg-Aussprache.ogg "File:Heidelberg-Aussprache.ogg"); [Palatine German](/wiki/Palatine_German_language "Palatine German language"): *Heidlberg*) is the [fifth-largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg_by_population "List of cities in Baden-Württemberg by population") in the [German state](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") of [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg"), and with a population of about 163,000, of which roughly a quarter consists of students,[[4]](#cite_note-4) it is [Germany's 51st-largest city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Germany_by_population "List of cities in Germany by population"). Located about 78 km (48 mi) south of [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), Heidelberg is part of the densely populated [Rhine-Neckar Metropolitan Region](/wiki/Rhine-Neckar "Rhine-Neckar") which has its centre in [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim").

Heidelberg is located on the [Neckar River](/wiki/Neckar_River "Neckar River"), at the point where it leaves its narrow valley between the [Oden Forest](/wiki/Oden_Forest "Oden Forest") and the [Little Oden Forest](/wiki/Kleiner_Odenwald "Kleiner Odenwald"), and enters the wide [Upper Rhine Plain](/wiki/Upper_Rhine_Plain "Upper Rhine Plain"). The old town lies in the valley, the end of which is flanked by the [Königstuhl](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigstuhl_(Odenwald) "Königstuhl (Odenwald)") in the south and the [Heiligenberg](/wiki/Heiligenberg_(Heidelberg) "Heiligenberg (Heidelberg)") in the north. The majority of the population lives in the districts west of the mountains in the Upper Rhine Plain, into which the city has expanded over time.

[Heidelberg University](/wiki/Heidelberg_University "Heidelberg University"), founded in 1386, is Germany's oldest and one of Europe's most reputable universities.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Heidelberg is a [scientific hub](/wiki/Science "Science") in Germany and home to several internationally renowned [research facilities](#Research) adjacent to its university, including the [European Molecular Biology Laboratory](/wiki/European_Molecular_Biology_Laboratory "European Molecular Biology Laboratory") and four [Max Planck Institutes](/wiki/Max_Planck_Society "Max Planck Society").[[6]](#cite_note-6) The city has also been a hub for the arts, especially literature, throughout the centuries, and it was designated a "[City of Literature](/wiki/City_of_Literature "City of Literature")" by the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [Creative Cities Network](/wiki/Creative_Cities_Network "Creative Cities Network").

Heidelberg was a seat of government of the former [Electorate of the Palatinate](/wiki/Electorate_of_the_Palatinate "Electorate of the Palatinate") and is a popular [tourist destination](/wiki/Tourism "Tourism") due to its [romantic](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") cityscape, including [Heidelberg Castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_Castle "Heidelberg Castle"), the [Philosophers' Walk](#Philosophers'_Walk), and the [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") old town.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/Heidelberg_Stadtteile_2012.svg/250px-Heidelberg_Stadtteile_2012.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Heidelberg_Stadtteile_2012.svg)

The districts of Heidelberg

Heidelberg is located on the eastern edge of the [Upper Rhine Plain](/wiki/Upper_Rhine_Plain "Upper Rhine Plain") ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Oberrheinebene*), at the place where the river [Neckar](/wiki/Neckar "Neckar") leaves its narrow valley through the [Odenwald](/wiki/Odenwald "Odenwald") mountains and begins the last leg of its journey across the plain towards [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim"), where it merges into the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") about 20 kilometers downstream.

A part of Heidelberg, including the historical old town and the famous [Heidelberg Castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_Castle "Heidelberg Castle"), is located in the narrow Neckar valley. Other parts (mostly quarters from around the 19th century or newer, or originally independent, later incorporated villages) sprawl out into the Rhine Plain and along the [Bergstraße](/wiki/Bergstra%C3%9Fe_(route) "Bergstraße (route)") (lit. 'mountain road'), the narrow strip of characteristic landscape along the sharp border between the plain and the Odenwald mountains.

The town is bordered by the [Königstuhl](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigstuhl_(Odenwald) "Königstuhl (Odenwald)") (568 m), the [Gaisberg](/wiki/Gaisberg_(Heidelberg) "Gaisberg (Heidelberg)") (375 m), and the [Heiligenberg](/wiki/Heiligenberg_(Heidelberg) "Heiligenberg (Heidelberg)") (445 m) mountains.

Heidelberg is on [European walking route E1](/wiki/European_walking_route_E1 "European walking route E1") (Sweden-[Umbria](/wiki/Umbria "Umbria")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Heidelberg_from_the_K%C3%B6nigstuhl.jpg/250px-Heidelberg_from_the_K%C3%B6nigstuhl.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heidelberg_from_the_K%C3%B6nigstuhl.jpg)

Heidelberg seen from [Königstuhl](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigstuhl_(Odenwald) "Königstuhl (Odenwald)")

### Flora and fauna

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Flora and fauna")]

Heidelberg is among the warmest regions of Germany, and plants atypical of the [central-European](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") climate flourish there, including [almond](/wiki/Almond "Almond") and [fig trees](/wiki/Common_fig "Common fig"), and many kinds of [palm trees](/wiki/Palm_trees "Palm trees") and [olive trees](/wiki/Olive_trees "Olive trees"). Alongside the *Philosophenweg* (Philosophers' Walk) on the opposite side of the Old Town, [winegrowing](/wiki/Winegrowing "Winegrowing") was restarted in 2000.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

There is a wild population of African [rose-ringed parakeets](/wiki/Rose-ringed_parakeet "Rose-ringed parakeet"),[[8]](#cite_note-8) and a wild population of [Siberian](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") [swan geese](/wiki/Swan_goose "Swan goose"), which can be seen mainly on the islands in the [Neckar](/wiki/Neckar "Neckar") near the district of Bergheim.

### Administrative structures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Administrative structures")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ea/Altstadt_Heidelberg.jpg/250px-Altstadt_Heidelberg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Altstadt_Heidelberg.jpg)

The Old Town

Heidelberg is a [unitary authority](/wiki/Unitary_authority#Germany "Unitary authority") within the [Regierungsbezirk Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe_(region) "Karlsruhe (region)"). The [Rhein-Neckar-Kreis](/wiki/Rhein-Neckar-Kreis "Rhein-Neckar-Kreis") rural district surrounds it and has its seat in the town, although the town is not a part of the district. Heidelberg is a part of the Rhine-Neckar Metropolitan Region, often referred to as the [Rhein-Neckar Triangle](/wiki/Rhein-Neckar-Triangle "Rhein-Neckar-Triangle").

This region consists of the southern part of the [State](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") of [Hessen](/wiki/Hessen "Hessen"), the southern part of the State of [Rhineland-Palatinate](/wiki/Rhineland-Palatinate "Rhineland-Palatinate") (*Vorderpfalz*), the administrative districts of [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim") and Heidelberg, and the municipalities of the [Rhein-Neckar-Kreis](/wiki/Rhein-Neckar-Kreis "Rhein-Neckar-Kreis"). The Rhein-Neckar Triangle became a European [metropolitan area](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area") in 2005.

Heidelberg consists of 15 districts distributed in six sectors of the town. In the central area are Altstadt (the Old Town), [Bergheim](/wiki/Heidelberg-Bergheim "Heidelberg-Bergheim") and [Weststadt](/wiki/Weststadt,_Heidelberg "Weststadt, Heidelberg"); in the north, Neuenheim and Handschuhsheim; in the east, [Ziegelhausen](/wiki/Heidelberg-Ziegelhausen "Heidelberg-Ziegelhausen") and Schlierbach; in the south, [Südstadt](/wiki/Heidelberg-S%C3%BCdstadt "Heidelberg-Südstadt"), [Rohrbach](/wiki/Rohrbach,_Heidelberg "Rohrbach, Heidelberg"), Emmertsgrund, and Boxberg; in the southwest, [Kirchheim](/wiki/Heidelberg-Kirchheim "Heidelberg-Kirchheim"); in the west, Pfaffengrund, Wieblingen, and a new district, named *Bahnstadt*, is built on land in Weststadt and Wieblingen. The new district will have approximately 5,000–6,000 residents and employment for 7,000. Further, new residential space for 10,000–15,000 residents was made available in Patrick Henry Village following the departure of the US Armed Forces.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Neighbouring communes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Neighbouring communes")]

The following towns and [communes](/wiki/Commune_(administrative_division) "Commune (administrative division)") border the city of Heidelberg, beginning in the west and in a clockwise direction: [Edingen-Neckarhausen](/wiki/Edingen-Neckarhausen "Edingen-Neckarhausen"), [Dossenheim](/wiki/Dossenheim "Dossenheim"), [Schriesheim](/wiki/Schriesheim "Schriesheim"), [Wilhelmsfeld](/wiki/Wilhelmsfeld "Wilhelmsfeld"), [Schönau](/wiki/Sch%C3%B6nau_(Odenwald) "Schönau (Odenwald)"), [Neckargemünd](/wiki/Neckargem%C3%BCnd "Neckargemünd"), [Bammental](/wiki/Bammental "Bammental"), [Gaiberg](/wiki/Gaiberg "Gaiberg"), [Leimen](/wiki/Leimen_(Baden) "Leimen (Baden)"), [Sandhausen](/wiki/Sandhausen "Sandhausen"), [Oftersheim](/wiki/Oftersheim "Oftersheim"), [Plankstadt](/wiki/Plankstadt "Plankstadt"), [Eppelheim](/wiki/Eppelheim "Eppelheim") (all part of the [Rhein-Neckar-Kreis](/wiki/Rhein-Neckar-Kreis "Rhein-Neckar-Kreis")), and [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim").

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Climate")]

Heidelberg has an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") *Cfb*), defined by the protected valley between the Pfälzerwald and the Odenwald. The almost year round warm temperatures are usually determined by [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic "Atlantic") air masses in the winter and increased influence from the western [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean") in summer.
In contrast to the nearby [Upper Rhine Plain](/wiki/Upper_Rhine_Plain "Upper Rhine Plain"), Heidelberg's position in the valley leads to more frequent easterly winds than average. The hillsides of the Odenwald favor clouding and precipitation.

Spring starts earlier than the average in Germany (usually mid February in the recent years). While the weather in spring is typically warm, it is also known to change far more often than in the summer.

Summer starts typically in June and stays until September. In this time the weather is typically stable, with single thunderstorms marking the only precipitation. Day temperatures of around 30 °C are typical, but can reach up to 40 °C during heat waves.

Autumn starts warm in September and cools down to typical winter temperatures for the region by the end of November. Precipitation levels begin to increase and, due to the decreasing sunlight, the region is affected by fog more frequently from the second part of October onwards.

Winters are mostly mild, though light nighttime frosts do occur in the coldest months. Snow is a rare event, and precipitation normally falls as rain. Winters are the wettest time of the year, with frequent western winds blowing from the Atlantic. Storms ("Orkane") can create severe damage, and the Neckar is often affected by floods.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

According to the [German Meteorological Service](/wiki/Deutscher_Wetterdienst "Deutscher Wetterdienst"), Heidelberg was the warmest place in Germany in 2009.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/Heidelberg_nachts.jpg/250px-Heidelberg_nachts.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heidelberg_nachts.jpg)

Heidelberg on the Neckar at night

Main article: [History of Heidelberg](/wiki/History_of_Heidelberg "History of Heidelberg")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Early history")]

Between 600,000 and 200,000 years ago,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] "[Heidelberg Man](/wiki/Homo_heidelbergensis "Homo heidelbergensis")" died at nearby [Mauer](/wiki/Mauer_(Baden) "Mauer (Baden)"). His jaw bone was discovered in 1907. Scientific dating determined his remains as the earliest evidence of human life in Europe. In the 5th century BC, a [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") fortress of refuge and place of worship were built on the *Heiligenberg*, or "Holy Mountain". Both places can still be identified. In 40 AD, a fort was built and occupied by the 24th Roman cohort and the 2nd [Cyrenaican](/wiki/Cyrenaican "Cyrenaican") cohort (CCG XXIIII and CCH II CYR). The late Roman Emperor [Valentinian I](/wiki/Valentinian_I "Valentinian I"), in 369 AD, built new and maintained older *[castra](/wiki/Castra "Castra")* (permanent camps) and a signal tower on the bank of the [Neckar](/wiki/Neckar "Neckar"). They built a wooden bridge based on stone pillars across it. The camp protected the first civilian settlements and was eventually captured by [Germanic tribes](/wiki/Germanic_people "Germanic people"). The local administrative centre in Roman times was the nearby city of Lopodunum, today known as [Ladenburg](/wiki/Ladenburg "Ladenburg").

### Middle Ages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Middle Ages")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Carl_Blechen_003.jpg/250px-Carl_Blechen_003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carl_Blechen_003.jpg)

[Heidelberg Castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_Castle "Heidelberg Castle"), here shown in a painting by [Carl Blechen](/wiki/Carl_Blechen "Carl Blechen"), was destroyed by the French during the [war of the succession](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War") of the [Electorate of the Palatinate](/wiki/Electorate_of_the_Palatinate "Electorate of the Palatinate").

Modern Heidelberg can trace its beginnings to the fifth century. The village *Bergheim* (Mountain Home) is first mentioned for that period in documents dated to 769 AD. *Bergheim* now lies in the middle of modern Heidelberg. The people gradually converted to Christianity. In 870 AD, the [monastery](/wiki/Monastery "Monastery") of [St. Michael](/wiki/St._Michael "St. Michael") was founded on the [Heiligenberg](/wiki/Heiligenberg_(Heidelberg) "Heiligenberg (Heidelberg)") inside the double rampart of the Celtic fortress. Around 1130, the Neuburg Monastery was founded in the Neckar valley. At the same time, the [bishopric](/wiki/Bishopric "Bishopric") of [Worms](/wiki/Worms,_Germany "Worms, Germany") extended its influence into the valley, founding [Schönau Abbey](/wiki/Sch%C3%B6nau_Abbey_(Odenwald) "Schönau Abbey (Odenwald)") in 1142. Modern Heidelberg can trace its roots to this 12th-century monastery. The first reference to Heidelberg can be found in a document in Schönau Abbey dated to 1196. This is considered to be the town's founding date. In 1156, Heidelberg castle and its neighboring settlement were taken over by the house of [Hohenstaufen](/wiki/Hohenstaufen "Hohenstaufen"). [Conrad of Hohenstaufen](/wiki/Conrad_of_Hohenstaufen "Conrad of Hohenstaufen") became [Count Palatine of the Rhine](/wiki/Count_Palatine_of_the_Rhine "Count Palatine of the Rhine") (German: *Pfalzgraf bei Rhein*). In 1195, the [Electorate of the Palatinate](/wiki/Electorate_of_the_Palatinate "Electorate of the Palatinate") passed to the [House of Welf](/wiki/House_of_Welf "House of Welf") through marriage. In 1214, [Ludwig I, Duke of Bavaria](/wiki/Louis_I,_Duke_of_Bavaria "Louis I, Duke of Bavaria") acquired the Palatinate, as a consequence of which the castle came under his control. By 1303, another castle had been constructed for defense. In 1356, the Counts Palatine were granted far-reaching rights in the [Golden Bull](/wiki/Golden_Bull_of_1356 "Golden Bull of 1356"), in addition to becoming [Electors](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector"). In 1386, [Heidelberg University](/wiki/Heidelberg_University "Heidelberg University") was founded by [Rupert I, Elector Palatine](/wiki/Rupert_I,_Elector_Palatine "Rupert I, Elector Palatine").[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Modern history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Modern history")]

Heidelberg University played a leading part in Medieval [Scholasticism](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism"), [Renaissance humanism](/wiki/Renaissance_humanism_in_Northern_Europe "Renaissance humanism in Northern Europe"), the [German Reformation](/wiki/German_Reformation "German Reformation"), and in the subsequent conflict between [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Calvinism](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") during the 15th and 16th centuries. In April 1518, a few months after proclaiming his *[Ninety-five Theses](/wiki/Ninety-five_Theses "Ninety-five Theses")*, [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") was received in Heidelberg, to defend them.

Heidelberg's library, founded in 1421, is the oldest existing public library in Germany.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

In 1537, the castle located higher up the mountain was destroyed by a gunpowder explosion. The duke's palace was built at the site of the lower castle.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Belagerung_Heidelbergs_C-L_205.jpg/250px-Belagerung_Heidelbergs_C-L_205.jpg)](/wiki/File:Belagerung_Heidelbergs_C-L_205.jpg)

The siege of Heidelberg 1622

[Elector Frederick III](/wiki/Elector_Frederick_III "Elector Frederick III"), sovereign of the [Electoral Palatinate](/wiki/Electoral_Palatinate "Electoral Palatinate") from 1559 to 1576, commissioned the composition of a new [Catechism](/wiki/Catechism "Catechism") for his territory. While the catechism's introduction credits the "entire theological faculty here" (at the [University of Heidelberg](/wiki/University_of_Heidelberg "University of Heidelberg")) and "all the superintendents and prominent servants of the church" for the composition of the Catechism, [Zacharius Ursinus](/wiki/Zacharias_Ursinus "Zacharias Ursinus") is commonly regarded as the catechism's principal author. [Caspar Olevianus](/wiki/Caspar_Olevianus "Caspar Olevianus") (1536–1587) was formerly asserted as a co-author of the document, though this theory has been largely discarded by modern scholarship. [Johann Sylvan](/wiki/Johann_Sylvan "Johann Sylvan"), [Adam Neuser](/wiki/Adam_Neuser "Adam Neuser"), Johannes Willing, [Thomas Erastus](/wiki/Thomas_Erastus "Thomas Erastus"), Michael Diller, Johannes Brunner, Tilemann Mumius, Petrus Macheropoeus, Johannes Eisenmenger, [Immanuel Tremellius](/wiki/Immanuel_Tremellius "Immanuel Tremellius"), and [Pierre Boquin](/wiki/Pierre_Boquin "Pierre Boquin") are all likely to have contributed to the Catechism in some way. Frederick himself wrote the preface to the Catechism and closely oversaw its composition and publication. Frederick, who was officially Lutheran but had strong Reformed leanings, wanted to even out the religious situation of his highly Lutheran territory within the primarily Catholic [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). The [Council of Trent](/wiki/Council_of_Trent "Council of Trent") had just concluded with its conclusions and decrees against the Protestant faiths, and the [Peace of Augsburg](/wiki/Peace_of_Augsburg "Peace of Augsburg") had only granted toleration for [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") within the empire where the ruler was Lutheran. One of the aims of the catechism was to counteract the teachings of the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") as well as [Anabaptists](/wiki/Anabaptism "Anabaptism") and "strict" [Gnesio-Lutherans](/wiki/Gnesio-Lutherans "Gnesio-Lutherans") like [Tilemann Heshusius](/wiki/Tilemann_Heshusius "Tilemann Heshusius") and [Matthias Flacius](/wiki/Matthias_Flacius "Matthias Flacius"), who were resisting Frederick's Reformed influences, particularly on the matter of Eucharist ([the Lord's Supper](/wiki/Eucharist "Eucharist")). The Catechism-based each of its statements on biblical [proof-texts](/wiki/Prooftext "Prooftext"), and Frederick himself would defend it as biblical, not reformed, at the 1566 [Diet of Augsburg](/wiki/Diet_of_Augsburg "Diet of Augsburg") when he was called to answer to charges of violating the Peace of Augsburg. This was the [Heidelberg Catechism](/wiki/Heidelberg_Catechism "Heidelberg Catechism"), officially called the ″Catechism, or Christian Instruction, according to the Usages of the Churches and Schools of the Electoral [Palatinate](/wiki/Palatinate_(region) "Palatinate (region)").″

In November 1619, the Royal [Crown of Bohemia](/wiki/Crown_of_Bohemia "Crown of Bohemia") was offered to the Elector, [Frederick V](/wiki/Frederick_V_of_the_Palatinate "Frederick V of the Palatinate"). (He was married to [Princess Elizabeth](/wiki/Elizabeth_of_Bohemia "Elizabeth of Bohemia"), eldest daughter of [James VI and I](/wiki/James_VI_and_I "James VI and I") of Scotland and England, respectively.) Frederick became known as the "Winter King", as he reigned for only one winter before the Imperial [House of Habsburg](/wiki/House_of_Habsburg "House of Habsburg") regained Bohemia by force. His overthrow in 1621 marked the beginning of the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War"). In 1622, after a [siege of two months](/wiki/Siege_of_Heidelberg_(1622) "Siege of Heidelberg (1622)"), the armies of the [Catholic League](/wiki/Catholic_League_(German) "Catholic League (German)"), commanded by [Johann Tserclaes, Count of Tilly](/wiki/Johann_Tserclaes,_Count_of_Tilly "Johann Tserclaes, Count of Tilly"), captured the town. Tilly gave the famous *[Bibliotheca Palatina](/wiki/Bibliotheca_Palatina "Bibliotheca Palatina")* from the [Church of the Holy Spirit](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Spirit,_Heidelberg "Church of the Holy Spirit, Heidelberg") to the Pope as a present. The Catholic and Bavarian [House of Wittelsbach](/wiki/House_of_Wittelsbach "House of Wittelsbach") gained control over the Palatinate and the title of Prince-Elector.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Hotelritter.jpg/250px-Hotelritter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hotelritter.jpg)

Hotel zum Ritter St. Georg

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Alte_Bruecke_Heidelberg_von_Konrad_Linck_1788.jpg/250px-Alte_Bruecke_Heidelberg_von_Konrad_Linck_1788.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alte_Bruecke_Heidelberg_von_Konrad_Linck_1788.jpg)

Old Bridge, Konrad Linck, 1788

In late 1634, after the Swedish army had conquered Heidelberg, imperial forces attempted to recapture the city. They quickly took the city, but were unable to take the castle. As they prepared to blow up its fortifications with gunpowder the French army arrived, 30,000 men strong, led by [Urbain de Maillé-Brézé](/wiki/Urbain_de_Maill%C3%A9-Br%C3%A9z%C3%A9 "Urbain de Maillé-Brézé"), who had fought in many battles and participated in the [Siege of La Rochelle](/wiki/Siege_of_La_Rochelle "Siege of La Rochelle") (1627–1628), and [Jacques-Nompar de Caumont, duc de La Force](/wiki/Jacques-Nompar_de_Caumont,_duc_de_La_Force "Jacques-Nompar de Caumont, duc de La Force"). They broke the siege and drove off the Imperial forces.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 1648, at the end of the war, Frederick V's son [Charles I Louis](/wiki/Charles_I_Louis "Charles I Louis"), Elector Palatine, was able to recover his titles and lands. To strengthen his dynasty, Charles I Louis arranged the marriage of his daughter [Liselotte](/wiki/Elizabeth_Charlotte,_Madame_Palatine "Elizabeth Charlotte, Madame Palatine") to [Philip I, Duke of Orléans](/wiki/Philip_I,_Duke_of_Orl%C3%A9ans "Philip I, Duke of Orléans"), brother of [Louis XIV](/wiki/Louis_XIV "Louis XIV"), King of France. In 1685, after the death of Charles Louis' son, Elector [Charles II](/wiki/Charles_II,_Elector_Palatine "Charles II, Elector Palatine"), King Louis XIV laid claim to his sister-in-law's inheritance. The Germans rejected the claim, in part because of religious differences between local Protestants and the French Catholics, as the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") had divided the peoples of Europe. The [War of the Grand Alliance](/wiki/War_of_the_Grand_Alliance "War of the Grand Alliance") ensued. In 1689, French troops took the town and castle, bringing nearly total destruction to the area in 1693. As a result of the destruction due to repeated French invasions related to the [War of the Palatinate Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Palatinate_Succession "War of the Palatinate Succession") coupled with severe winters, thousands of German Calvinist Palatines emigrated in the early 18th century. They fled to other European cities and especially to London (where the refugees were called "the poor Palatines"). In sympathy for the Protestants, in 1709–1710, [Queen Anne's](/wiki/Anne,_Queen_of_Great_Britain "Anne, Queen of Great Britain") government arranged transport for nearly 6,000 Palatines to New York. Others were transported to [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"), and to [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina"). They worked their passage and later settled in the English colonies there.

In 1720, after assigning a major church for exclusively Catholic use, religious conflicts with the mostly Protestant inhabitants of Heidelberg caused the Roman Catholic Prince-Elector [Charles III Philip](/wiki/Charles_III_Philip "Charles III Philip") to transfer his court to nearby [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim"). The court remained there until the Elector [Charles Theodore](/wiki/Charles_Theodore,_Elector_of_Bavaria "Charles Theodore, Elector of Bavaria") became [Elector of Bavaria](/wiki/Rulers_of_Bavaria "Rulers of Bavaria") in 1777 and established his court in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich"). In 1742, Elector Charles Theodore began rebuilding the Palace. In 1764, a lightning bolt destroyed other palace buildings during reconstruction, causing the work to be discontinued.

### 1803 to 1933

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 1803 to 1933")]

Heidelberg fell to the [Grand Duchy of Baden](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Baden "Grand Duchy of Baden") in 1803. [Charles Frederick, Grand Duke of Baden](/wiki/Charles_Frederick,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Charles Frederick, Grand Duke of Baden"), re-founded the university, named "Ruperto-Carola" after its two founders. Notable scholars soon earned it a reputation as a "royal residence of the intellect". In the 18th century, the town was rebuilt in the Baroque style on the old medieval layout.

In 1810 the [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") refugee Count Charles Graimberg began to preserve the palace [ruins](/wiki/Ruins "Ruins") and establish a historical collection. In 1815, the emperor of [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria"), the emperor of Russia and the king of [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") formed the "[Holy Alliance](/wiki/Holy_Alliance "Holy Alliance")" in Heidelberg. In 1848, the German National Assembly was held there. In 1849, during the Palatinate-Baden rebellion of the [1848 Revolutions](/wiki/Revolutions_of_1848_in_the_German_states "Revolutions of 1848 in the German states"), Heidelberg was the headquarters of a revolutionary army. It was defeated by a Prussian army near Waghaeusel. The city was occupied by Prussian troops until 1850. Between 1920 and 1933, Heidelberg University became a center of notable physicians Czerny, Erb, and Krehl; and humanists Rohde, Weber, and Gundolf.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Nazism and the World War II period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Nazism and the World War II period")]

During the Nazi period (1933–1945), Heidelberg was a stronghold of the [NSDAP](/wiki/NSDAP "NSDAP")/Nazi party (the National Socialist German Workers' Party), the strongest party in the elections before 1933 (the NSDAP obtained 30% at the communal elections of 1930[[21]](#cite_note-21)). The NSDAP received 45.9% of the votes in the [German federal election of March 1933](/wiki/German_federal_election,_March_1933 "German federal election, March 1933") (the national average was 43.9%).[[22]](#cite_note-22)
In 1934 and 1935 the *Reichsarbeitsdienst* (State Labor Service) and Heidelberg University students built the huge [*Thingstätte* amphitheatre](/wiki/Heidelberg_Thingst%C3%A4tte "Heidelberg Thingstätte") on the *Heiligenberg* north of the town, for [Nazi Party](/wiki/Nazi_Party "Nazi Party") and [SS](/wiki/SS "SS") events. A few months later, the inauguration of the huge Ehrenfriedhof memorial cemetery completed the second and last NSDAP project in Heidelberg. This cemetery is on the southern side of the old part of town, a little south of the [Königstuhl](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigstuhl_(Odenwald) "Königstuhl (Odenwald)") hilltop, and faces west towards France. During World War II and after, [Wehrmacht](/wiki/Wehrmacht "Wehrmacht") soldiers were buried there.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Heidelberger_Synagogenplatz_2.JPG/250px-Heidelberger_Synagogenplatz_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Heidelberger_Synagogenplatz_2.JPG)

Memorial stone marking the site of the synagogue in the Lauerstrasse

During the *[Kristallnacht](/wiki/Kristallnacht "Kristallnacht")* on 9 November 1938, Nazis burned down synagogues at two locations in the city. The next day, they started the systematic deportation of Jews, sending 150 to [Dachau concentration camp](/wiki/Dachau_concentration_camp "Dachau concentration camp"). On 22 October 1940, during the "Wagner Buerckel event", the Nazis deported 6000 local Jews, including 281 from Heidelberg, to [Camp Gurs](/wiki/Camp_Gurs "Camp Gurs") concentration camp in France. Within a few months, as many as 1000 of them (201 from Heidelberg) died of hunger and disease.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Among the deportees from Heidelberg, the poet [Alfred Mombert](/wiki/Alfred_Mombert "Alfred Mombert") (1872–1942) left the [concentration camp](/wiki/Nazi_concentration_camp "Nazi concentration camp") in April 1941 thanks to the Swiss poet Hans Reinhart but died shortly thereafter due to illness he contracted while held prisoner.[[24]](#cite_note-24) From 1942, the deportees who had survived internment in Gurs were deported to Eastern Europe, where most of them were murdered.

The U.S. [44th Infantry Division](/wiki/44th_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "44th Infantry Division (United States)") took part in combat in Western Europe throughout 1944 and early 1945, and the division's artillery commander, Brigadier General [William A. Beiderlinden](/wiki/William_A._Beiderlinden "William A. Beiderlinden"), became the subject of international headlines in March 1945, when he helped save Heidelberg from bombing by persuading Nazi troops to withdraw.[[25]](#cite_note-St._Joseph_News-Press-1981-25) When his command was ordered to shell the city, Beiderlinden took the initiative to contact the [burgomaster](/wiki/Burgomaster "Burgomaster") and attempt to persuade Nazi soldiers to abandon their positions.[[25]](#cite_note-St._Joseph_News-Press-1981-25) Though burgomasters were forbidden from conducting such talks, Heidelberg's burgomaster ignored warnings from the local Nazi [gauleiter](/wiki/Gauleiter "Gauleiter") and discussed the matter with Beiderlinden.[[25]](#cite_note-St._Joseph_News-Press-1981-25) The negotiations focused on the importance of sparing [Heidelberg University](/wiki/Heidelberg_University "Heidelberg University") and other historic and culturally significant sites.[[25]](#cite_note-St._Joseph_News-Press-1981-25) Beiderlinden and the burgomaster agreed to terms, and the Nazis spared the city by evacuating.[[25]](#cite_note-St._Joseph_News-Press-1981-25)

On 29 March 1945, German troops left the city after destroying three arches of the old bridge, Heidelberg's treasured river crossing. They also destroyed the more modern bridge downstream. The U.S. Army ([63rd Infantry](/wiki/63rd_Infantry_Division_(United_States) "63rd Infantry Division (United States)"), 7th Army) entered the town on 30 March 1945. The civilian population surrendered without resistance.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Heidelberg, unlike most German cities and towns, was spared from Allied bombing raids during the war. A popular belief is that Heidelberg escaped bombing because the U.S. Army wanted to use the city as a garrison after the war, but, as Heidelberg was neither an industrial center nor a transport hub, it did not present a tactical or strategic target. Other notable university towns, such as [Tübingen](/wiki/T%C3%BCbingen "Tübingen") and [Göttingen](/wiki/G%C3%B6ttingen "Göttingen"), were spared bombing as well. Allied air raids focused extensively on the nearby industrial cities of [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim") and [Ludwigshafen](/wiki/Ludwigshafen "Ludwigshafen").

The U.S. Army may have chosen Heidelberg as a garrison base because of its excellent infrastructure, including the Heidelberg–Mannheim [Autobahn](/wiki/Autobahn "Autobahn") (motorway), which connected to the Mannheim–Darmstadt–Frankfurt Autobahn, and the U.S. Army installations in [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim") and [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"). The intact rail infrastructure was more important in the late 1940s and early 1950s when most heavy loads were still carried by train, not by truck. Heidelberg had the untouched Wehrmacht barracks, the "Grossdeutschland Kaserne" which the US Army occupied soon after, renaming it the [Campbell Barracks](/wiki/Campbell_Barracks "Campbell Barracks").

### History after 1945

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: History after 1945")]

In 1945, the university was reopened relatively quickly on the initiative of a small group of professors, among whom were the anti-Nazi economist [Alfred Weber](/wiki/Alfred_Weber "Alfred Weber") and the philosopher [Karl Jaspers](/wiki/Karl_Jaspers "Karl Jaspers").[[27]](#cite_note-27) The surgeon Karl Heinrich Bauer was nominated rector.

On 9 December 1945, US Army General [George S. Patton](/wiki/George_S._Patton "George S. Patton") was involved in a car accident in the adjacent city of [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim") and died in the Heidelberg US Army hospital on 21 December 1945. His funeral ceremony was held at the Heidelberg-Weststadt *Christuskirche* (Christ Church), and he was buried in the 3rd Army cemetery in [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg").[[28]](#cite_note-28)

During the post-war [military occupation](/wiki/Military_occupation "Military occupation"), the [U.S. Army](/wiki/U.S._Army "U.S. Army") used the *[Thingstätte](/wiki/Heidelberg_Thingst%C3%A4tte "Heidelberg Thingstätte")* for cultural and religious events. Civilian use started in the early to mid-1980s for occasional concerts and other cultural events. Today, the celebrations on *Hexennacht* ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language") for 'Witches' Night'), also called [Walpurgis Night](/wiki/Walpurgis_Night "Walpurgis Night"), the night of 30 April, are a regular "underground" fixture at the *Thingstätte*. Thousands of mostly young people congregate there to drum, to breathe fire, and to juggle. The event has gained fame throughout the region, as well as a certain notoriety due to the amount of litter left behind. Officially, this event is forbidden due to security concerns. The City declares it will fence the Thingstätte and prosecute any trespassers.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Population")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Heidelberg_population.svg/250px-Heidelberg_population.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Heidelberg_population.svg)

Population growth

The population of the city of Heidelberg exceeded 100,000 for the first time in 1946. It is a city with an international population, including one of the largest American communities outside North America, but this is not analysed in the Heidelberg population statistics. At the end of December 2011, the city had 149,633 inhabitants with an official primary residence in Heidelberg (not including the soldiers and employees of the U.S. Army and their dependents, a total of about 20,000 people), a historic high.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The following table shows the number of inhabitants within the boundaries of the city at the time. To 1833 they are mostly estimates, then census results or official updates of the statistical offices of the time or the city administration. The data refer from 1843 to the "local population", from 1925 to the resident population and since 1987 the "population at the site of their main dwelling." Prior to 1843 the population was determined by non-uniform collection procedures.

Heidelberg currently has a population of about 160,000 and is the 5th largest city in [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg"). About 37,000 (24% of the population) are students, which makes Heidelberg one of the largest university cities in Germany. Heidelberg's population has grown since 1945 (after WWII) and has almost never declined due to the popularity of this city, where many workers, students, and entrepreneurs have moved to Heidelberg.

## Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Politics")]

Since 2006, the *Oberbürgermeister* (lord mayor) of Heidelberg has been the independent Eckart Würzner. From 1990 to 2006, the mayor was Beate Weber ([SPD](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany")).

The council consists of 48 members with the mayor as chairman. The council is directly elected for a term of five years. The task of the council is to decide all the affairs of the city, with the mayor presiding. The council controls the city administration and oversees the enforcement of its decisions.

Heidelberg has always been a stronghold of [The Greens](/wiki/Alliance_90/The_Greens "Alliance 90/The Greens"). For the municipal elections in 2009, they split into the Green Alternative List and Alliance 90/The Greens and each ran their own lists. Together they gained 10 seats to become the strongest force for the first time. In September 2011, two members of the GAL Group joined the Alliance 90/The Greens, so that together with the members of generation.hd, they formed the largest group in the council.

For elections to the [Bundestag](/wiki/Bundestag "Bundestag"), the city is part of the [Heidelberg](/wiki/Heidelberg_(electoral_district) "Heidelberg (electoral district)") [constituency](/wiki/List_of_Bundestag_constituencies "List of Bundestag constituencies").

## Cityscape

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Cityscape")]

### The old town

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: The old town")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/2002-04-02_Heidelberger_Rathaus_IMG_0388.jpg/250px-2002-04-02_Heidelberger_Rathaus_IMG_0388.jpg)](/wiki/File:2002-04-02_Heidelberger_Rathaus_IMG_0388.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Heidelberg_winter.jpg/250px-Heidelberg_winter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heidelberg_winter.jpg)

View from the castle during winter, 2014

The "old town" (German: *Altstadt*), on the south bank of the [Neckar](/wiki/Neckar "Neckar"), is long and narrow. It is dominated by the ruins of [Heidelberg Castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_Castle "Heidelberg Castle"), 80 metres above the Neckar on the steep wooded slopes of the *[Königstuhl](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigstuhl_(Odenwald) "Königstuhl (Odenwald)")* (King's chair or throne) hill.

* The Main Street (*Hauptstrasse*), a mile-long pedestrian street, running the length of the old town.
* The [old stone bridge](/wiki/Old_Bridge_(Heidelberg) "Old Bridge (Heidelberg)") was erected 1786–1788. A medieval bridge gate is on the side of the old town, and was originally part of the town wall. Baroque tower helmets were added as part of the erection of the stone bridge in 1788.
* The [Church of the Holy Spirit](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Spirit,_Heidelberg "Church of the Holy Spirit, Heidelberg") (*Heiliggeistkirche*), a [late Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") church in the marketplace of the old town.
* The Karls' gate (*Karlstor*) is a triumphal arch in honour of the Prince Elector Karl Theodor, located at Heidelberg's east side. It was built 1775–1781 and designed by Nicolas de Pigage.
* The house *Zum Ritter Sankt Georg* (Knight St. George) is one of the few buildings to survive the War of Succession. Standing across from the Church of the Holy Spirit, it was built in the style of the late [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance "Renaissance"). It is named after the sculpture at the top.
* The *Marstall* (Stables), a 16th-century building on the Neckar that has served several purposes through its history. It is now a cafeteria for the university.

### Heidelberg Castle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Heidelberg Castle")]

Main article: [Heidelberg Castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_Castle "Heidelberg Castle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Plan_des_Heidelberger_Schlosses_von_Peter_Friedrich_von_Walpergen_um_1780.jpg/250px-Plan_des_Heidelberger_Schlosses_von_Peter_Friedrich_von_Walpergen_um_1780.jpg)](/wiki/File:Plan_des_Heidelberger_Schlosses_von_Peter_Friedrich_von_Walpergen_um_1780.jpg)

Historic map of [Heidelberg Castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_Castle "Heidelberg Castle")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/07/Heidelberg_Schloss_Luftbild_2.JPG/250px-Heidelberg_Schloss_Luftbild_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Heidelberg_Schloss_Luftbild_2.JPG)

Aerial view of Heidelberg Castle

The castle is a mix of styles from [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") to [Renaissance](/wiki/Renaissance_architecture "Renaissance architecture"). Prince Elector Ruprecht III (1398–1410) erected the first building in the inner courtyard as a royal residence. The building was divided into a ground floor made of stone and framework upper levels. Another royal building is located opposite the Ruprecht Building: the Fountain Hall. Prince Elector Philipp (1476–1508) is said to have arranged the transfer of the hall's columns from a decayed palace of [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") from [Ingelheim](/wiki/Ingelheim "Ingelheim") to Heidelberg.

In the 16th and 17th centuries, the Prince Electors added two palace buildings and turned the fortress into a castle. The two dominant buildings at the eastern and northern side of the courtyard were erected during the rule of Ottheinrich (1556–1559) and Friedrich IV (1583–1610). Under Friedrich V (1613–1619), the main building of the west side was erected, the so-called "English Building".

The castle and its garden were destroyed several times during the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War") and the Palatine War of Succession. As Prince Elector Karl Theodor tried to restore the castle, lightning struck in 1764, and ended all attempts at rebuilding. Later on, the castle was misused as a quarry; stones from the castle were taken to build new houses in Heidelberg. This was stopped in 1800 by Count Charles de Graimberg, who then began the process of preserving the castle.

Although the interior is in Gothic style, the King's Hall was not built until 1934. Today, the hall is used for festivities, e.g. dinner banquets, balls and theatre performances. During the Heidelberg Castle Festival in the summer, the courtyard is the site of open air musicals, operas, theatre performances, and classical concerts performed by the Heidelberg Philharmonics.

The castle is surrounded by a park, where the famous poet [Johann von Goethe](/wiki/Johann_Wolfgang_von_Goethe "Johann Wolfgang von Goethe") once walked. The [Heidelberger Bergbahn](/wiki/Heidelberger_Bergbahn "Heidelberger Bergbahn") [funicular](/wiki/Funicular "Funicular") railway runs from Kornmakt to the summit of the Königstuhl via the castle.

The castle looks over the entire city of Heidelberg and the Neckar Valley.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Philosophers' Walk

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Philosophers' Walk")]

On the northern side of the Neckar is located the Heiligenberg (*Saints' Mountain*), along the side of which runs the Philosophers' Walk (German: *Philosophenweg*), with scenic views of the old town and castle. Traditionally, Heidelberg's philosophers and university professors would walk and talk along the pathway. Farther up the mountain lie the ruined 11th-century [Monastery of St. Michael](/wiki/Monastery_of_St._Michael_(Heidelberg) "Monastery of St. Michael (Heidelberg)"), the smaller Monastery of St. Stephen, a Nazi-era amphitheater, the so-called Pagan's hole and the remains of an earthen [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") [hill fort](/wiki/Hill_fort "Hill fort") from the 4th century BC.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/a/a9/View_on_the_old_Heidelberg.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_on_the_old_Heidelberg.jpg)

View from the so-called "Philosophers' Walk" (German: *Philosophenweg*) towards the Old Town, with [Heidelberg Castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_Castle "Heidelberg Castle"), [Heiliggeist Church](/wiki/Heidelberg_Heiliggeist_church "Heidelberg Heiliggeist church") and the Old Bridge

### Heidelberg churches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Heidelberg churches")]

There are many historical churches in Heidelberg and its surroundings. The [Church of the Holy Spirit](/wiki/Church_of_the_Holy_Spirit,_Heidelberg "Church of the Holy Spirit, Heidelberg") has been shared over the centuries since the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") by both Catholics and Protestants. It is one of the few buildings to survive the many wars during the past centuries. It was rebuilt after the French set fire to it in 1709 during the [War of the Palatinian Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Grand_Alliance "War of the Grand Alliance"). The church has remains of the tombs and epitaphs of the past Palatinate electors. This Church stands in the *Marktplatz* next to the seat of local government. In 1720, Karl III Philip, Elector Palatine came into conflict with the town's Protestants as a result of giving the Church of the Holy Spirit exclusively to the Catholics for their use. It had previously been split by a partition and used by both congregations. Due to pressure by the mostly Protestant powers of Prussia, Holland, and Sweden, Prince Karl III Philip gave way and repartitioned the church for joint use. In 1936 the separating wall was removed. The church is now exclusively used by Protestants. Furthermore, there is the Catholic Church of the Jesuits. Its construction began in 1712. It was completed with the addition of a bell tower from 1866 to 1872. The church is also home to the Museum für sakrale Kunst und Liturgie (Museum of [Ecclesiastical](/wiki/Ecclesiastical "Ecclesiastical") Arts). The oldest church in Heidelberg is the St. Peter's Church (now [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran")). It was built some time during the 12th century.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Education")]

### Universities and academia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Universities and academia")]

Main articles: [Heidelberg University](/wiki/Heidelberg_University "Heidelberg University") and [List of Heidelberg University people](/wiki/List_of_Heidelberg_University_people "List of Heidelberg University people")

Heidelberg is known for its institutions of higher education. The most famous of those is [Heidelberg University](/wiki/Heidelberg_University "Heidelberg University"). Founded in 1386, it is one of Europe's oldest institutions. In fact, Heidelberg is the oldest [university town](/wiki/College_town "College town") of today's Germany. Among the prominent thinkers associated with the institution are [Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel](/wiki/Georg_Wilhelm_Friedrich_Hegel "Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel"), [Karl Jaspers](/wiki/Karl_Jaspers "Karl Jaspers"), [Hans-Georg Gadamer](/wiki/Hans-Georg_Gadamer "Hans-Georg Gadamer"), [Jürgen Habermas](/wiki/J%C3%BCrgen_Habermas "Jürgen Habermas"), [Karl-Otto Apel](/wiki/Karl-Otto_Apel "Karl-Otto Apel"), and [Hannah Arendt](/wiki/Hannah_Arendt "Hannah Arendt"). The campus is situated in two urban areas and several buildings. In numerous historical buildings in the old town there are the Faculties of the [Humanities](/wiki/Humanities "Humanities"), the [Social Science](/wiki/Social_Science "Social Science"), and the Faculty of [Law](/wiki/Jurisprudence "Jurisprudence"). The school of applied sciences is located in the *Science Tower* in Wieblingen. The Faculties of Medicine and [Natural Science](/wiki/Natural_Science "Natural Science") are settled on the Neuenheimer Feld Campus.

The campus of Heidelberg University has a total undergraduate enrollment of 30,898 as of 2014. The enrollment rate of this university is 16.3 percent. Less than 20 percent of the total student body is international. This university has many areas of study for national students such as; theology, law, philosophy, modern languages, economics, and social sciences. The university does not charge students for tuition. The school's academic calendar is semester based, and the majority of the language for instruction is in German. For international students the academic calendar is based on a block schedule. The international students attend in block periods of 5 weeks. The University or "Uni" is spread across three campuses each containing different fields of study.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Heidlbergaula.JPG/250px-Heidlbergaula.JPG)](/wiki/File:Heidlbergaula.JPG)

Old university hall

Since 1904 there has been a College of Educational Science, the *Pädagogische Hochschule Heidelberg*; since 1979 there has been a college of Jewish Studies, the *[Hochschule für Jüdische Studien Heidelberg](/wiki/Hochschule_f%C3%BCr_J%C3%BCdische_Studien_Heidelberg "Hochschule für Jüdische Studien Heidelberg")*. It comprises nine branches specializing in both religion and [Jewish culture](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"). The [Schiller International University](/wiki/Schiller_International_University "Schiller International University"), a private American university is also represented with a campus in Heidelberg offering several undergraduate and graduate programs in the fields of International Business and International Relations and Diplomacy.

### Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Research")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/EMBL_campus_Heidelberg.jpg/250px-EMBL_campus_Heidelberg.jpg)](/wiki/File:EMBL_campus_Heidelberg.jpg)

Buildings of [European Molecular Biology Laboratory](/wiki/European_Molecular_Biology_Laboratory "European Molecular Biology Laboratory") in Heidelberg, including the new Advanced Training Centre

In addition to the research centers and institutes of the university, there are numerous research institutions situated in the city of Heidelberg. Among them are the [European Molecular Biology Laboratory](/wiki/European_Molecular_Biology_Laboratory "European Molecular Biology Laboratory") (EMBL), [European Molecular Biology Organization](/wiki/European_Molecular_Biology_Organization "European Molecular Biology Organization") (EMBO), the [German Cancer Research Center](/wiki/German_Cancer_Research_Center "German Cancer Research Center") (DKFZ), [Max Planck Institute for Medical Research](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Medical_Research "Max Planck Institute for Medical Research"), [Max Planck Institute for Astronomy](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Astronomy "Max Planck Institute for Astronomy"), [Max Planck Institute for Nuclear Physics](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Nuclear_Physics "Max Planck Institute for Nuclear Physics"), [Max Planck Institute for Comparative Public Law and International Law](/wiki/Max_Planck_Institute_for_Comparative_Public_Law_and_International_Law "Max Planck Institute for Comparative Public Law and International Law").

### Schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Schools")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Fachhochschule_Heidelberg.jpg/250px-Fachhochschule_Heidelberg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fachhochschule_Heidelberg.jpg)

SRH Hochschule Heidelberg is one of the oldest and largest private universities in Germany.

Heidelberg is home to 23 elementary schools. There are several institutions of secondary education, both public and private, representing all levels of the [German school system](/wiki/German_school_system "German school system"). There are 14 [Gymnasiums](/wiki/Gymnasium_(Germany) "Gymnasium (Germany)"), with six of them private. With 52% of secondary students attending a Gymnasium, Heidelberg sits above the German average, perhaps because a large number of academics live in Heidelberg and its environs.

The gymnasiums include the [Kurfürst-Friedrich-Gymnasium](/w/index.php?title=Kurf%C3%BCrst-Friedrich-Gymnasium&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kurfürst-Friedrich-Gymnasium (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kurf%C3%BCrst-Friedrich-Gymnasium_Heidelberg "de:Kurfürst-Friedrich-Gymnasium Heidelberg")], [Bunsen-Gymnasium](/w/index.php?title=Bunsen-Gymnasium&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bunsen-Gymnasium (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bunsen-Gymnasium_Heidelberg "de:Bunsen-Gymnasium Heidelberg")], [Helmholtz-Gymnasium](/wiki/Helmholtz-Gymnasium_Heidelberg "Helmholtz-Gymnasium Heidelberg"), Hölderlin-Gymnasium and Elisabeth-von-Thadden-Schule. Then there are seven [Realschule](/wiki/Realschule "Realschule"), ten [Hauptschule](/wiki/Hauptschule "Hauptschule"), and nine vocational schools (the so-called *[Berufsschule](/wiki/Berufsschule "Berufsschule")*). In addition, there are several [folk high schools](/wiki/Folk_high_schools "Folk high schools") with different specialisations.[[34]](#cite_note-34) [Heidelberg International School](/wiki/Heidelberg_International_School "Heidelberg International School") serves the local expatriate community.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Economy")]

### Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Tourism")]

In 2004, 81.8% of people worked for service industries, including tourism. As a relic of the period of Romanticism, Heidelberg has been labeled a "Romantic town". This is used to attract more than [11.9 million visitors](https://www.heidelberg.de/english/Home/Life/Heidelberg+in+figures.html#:~:text=11.9%20million%20visitors.,11.9%20million%20visitors%20every%20year.) every year. Many events are organized to attract visitors. One of the biggest tourist attractions is the Christmas market during the winter time.

### Industry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Industry")]

Only 18% of employment is provided by industry. Printing and publishing are important enterprises; nearby [Walldorf](/wiki/Walldorf "Walldorf") is a center of the IT industry and [SAP](/wiki/SAP_SE "SAP SE") World Headquarters. Noted pen manufacturer [Lamy](/wiki/Lamy "Lamy") has its headquarters and factory in Heidelberg-Wieblingen. [Heidelberger Druckmaschinen](/wiki/Heidelberger_Druckmaschinen "Heidelberger Druckmaschinen") has its headquarters here; its factory is located in Walldorf. Soft-drink company Wild-Werke, manufacturer of the [Capri-Sonne](/wiki/Capri-Sonne "Capri-Sonne") ([Capri-Sun](/wiki/Capri-Sun "Capri-Sun") in the U.S.) is located nearby in Eppelheim. Heidelberg is also home to the headquarters of [HeidelbergCement](/wiki/HeidelbergCement "HeidelbergCement"), the world's second largest cement producer. The company has its roots in the suburb of Leimen where one of its cement plants is still located. With its long Hauptstraße, Heidelberg is a shopping destination for people from the surrounding smaller towns.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/Heidelberger_Druckmaschinen.jpg/250px-Heidelberger_Druckmaschinen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heidelberger_Druckmaschinen.jpg)

Print Media Academy

### Roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Roads")]

The [A 5 autobahn](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_5 "Bundesautobahn 5") runs through the western outskirts of Heidelberg, connecting the region to [Frankfurt am Main](/wiki/Frankfurt_am_Main "Frankfurt am Main") in the north and [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe "Karlsruhe") to the south. The [A 656](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_656 "Bundesautobahn 656") commences just west of the city, connecting Heidelberg with [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim"). Both highways meet at Heidelberg autobahn intersection in the city of Heidelberg, and the A 656 connects to the [A 6](/wiki/Bundesautobahn_6 "Bundesautobahn 6") at the Mannheim autobahn intersection, which connects to the east towards [Stuttgart](/wiki/Stuttgart "Stuttgart").

Furthermore, the [B 3](/wiki/Bundesstra%C3%9Fe_3 "Bundesstraße 3") (Frankfurt–Karlsruhe) runs north–south through the town, and the B 37 (Mannheim–[Eberbach](/wiki/Eberbach_(Baden) "Eberbach (Baden)")) runs east–west. Both meet in the city center at the Bismarckplatz. The [B 535](/w/index.php?title=Bundesstra%C3%9Fe_535&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bundesstraße 535 (page does not exist)") begin in the south of Heidelberg and runs to Schwetzingen.

#### Tourist roads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Tourist roads")]

Heidelberg is located on four tourist roads: [Bergstraße](/wiki/Bergstra%C3%9Fe_Route "Bergstraße Route"), [Bertha Benz Memorial Route](/wiki/Bertha_Benz_Memorial_Route "Bertha Benz Memorial Route"), [Castle Road](/wiki/Castle_Road "Castle Road"), and Straße der Demokratie (Road of Democracy).

### Railways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Railways")]

[Heidelberg Central Station](/wiki/Heidelberg_Central_Station "Heidelberg Central Station") (Hauptbahnhof) is on the [Rhine Valley Railway](/wiki/Rhine_Valley_Railway "Rhine Valley Railway") and is served by [Intercity-Express](/wiki/Intercity-Express "Intercity-Express"), [Euro City](/wiki/EuroCity_in_Germany "EuroCity in Germany") trains. This station is served by the [RheinNeckar S-Bahn](/wiki/RheinNeckar_S-Bahn "RheinNeckar S-Bahn"). There is also a station for [intercity bus services](/wiki/Intercity_bus_service "Intercity bus service") outside the central station.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### Public transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Public transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e1/Heidelberg_-_EC_-_2018-07-20_12-36-59.jpg/250px-Heidelberg_-_EC_-_2018-07-20_12-36-59.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heidelberg_-_EC_-_2018-07-20_12-36-59.jpg)

DB train [Heidelberg Hauptbahnhof](/wiki/Heidelberg_Hauptbahnhof "Heidelberg Hauptbahnhof")

The main transport hub of Heidelberg is the Bismarckplatz. Several main thoroughfares of the city intersect here and one of the longest pedestrian streets in Europe, the Hauptstraße (main street) runs from here through the entire old town of Heidelberg. Heidelberg Central Station was nearby for many years, which was a combined terminal and through station. In 1955, it was moved about 1.5 km further to the west, which removed the necessity for trains continuing north or south to reverse. The new central station became the second major transport hub of Heidelberg.

Heidelberg has had a public transport service since 1883, when horse-drawn trams were established. Due to the rapidly rising patronage it was decided on 20 December 1901 to convert the [Heidelberg tramway network](/wiki/Trams_in_Heidelberg "Trams in Heidelberg") to electrical operation. On 16 March 1902, the first electric tram ran on Rohrbacher Straße, sharing use of the suburban tracks built by the *Deutsche Eisenbahn-Gesellschaft* in 1901 between Heidelberg and [Wiesloch](/wiki/Wiesloch "Wiesloch"). Until the 1950s, the tram network was expanded a bit at a time. The rapidly growing popularity of car transport presented the operator of the trams with increasingly difficult problems and the tram network was gradually dismantled. It was not until 10 December 2006 that the network was extended again with the opening of a new tram line from [Kirchheim](/wiki/Heidelberg-Kirchheim "Heidelberg-Kirchheim"). Tram and bus services are now operated by [Rhein-Neckar-Verkehr](/wiki/Rhein-Neckar-Verkehr "Rhein-Neckar-Verkehr") (RNV). Since 1989, all fares are set under a uniform scheme by the [Verkehrsverbund Rhein-Neckar](/wiki/Verkehrsverbund_Rhein-Neckar "Verkehrsverbund Rhein-Neckar") (Rhine-Neckar Transport Association, VRN). [Carsharing](/wiki/Carsharing "Carsharing") increasingly provides a complement to public transport. More than 50 car-sharing stations are available to users in 12 of the 14 districts of Heidelberg offering a total of more than 100 cars.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Heidelberg_-_Bombardier_RNV6_-_RNV_4144_und_RNV_4140_-_2016-08-25_14-39-04.jpg/250px-Heidelberg_-_Bombardier_RNV6_-_RNV_4144_und_RNV_4140_-_2016-08-25_14-39-04.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heidelberg_-_Bombardier_RNV6_-_RNV_4144_und_RNV_4140_-_2016-08-25_14-39-04.jpg)

Trams in Heidelberg

Since 14 December 2003, Heidelberg has been connected to the network of the [Rhine-Neckar S-Bahn](/wiki/Rhine-Neckar_S-Bahn "Rhine-Neckar S-Bahn"), which opens up the entire Rhine-Neckar region, with lines connecting with the [Palatinate](/wiki/Palatinate_(region) "Palatinate (region)"), the [Saarland](/wiki/Saarland "Saarland"), and southern [Hesse](/wiki/Hesse "Hesse").

The [Heidelberger Bergbahn](/wiki/Heidelberger_Bergbahn "Heidelberger Bergbahn") (Heidelberg Mountain Railway) has run since 2005 with new cars on the lower part from Kornmarkt to Molkenkur and historic cars built in 1907 on the upper section of the [funicular](/wiki/Funicular "Funicular") from Molkenkur to [Königstuhl](/wiki/K%C3%B6nigstuhl_(Odenwald) "Königstuhl (Odenwald)"). It is one of the most popular means to reach [Heidelberg Castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_Castle "Heidelberg Castle"). The first plans for the funicular were drawn up in 1873. Due to a lack of funds, the first section of the funicular was not opened until 1890. In 2004, the upper section of the funicular was listed as part of the heritage of the state of Baden-Württemberg.

### United States military installations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: United States military installations")]

See also: [United States Army Garrison Heidelberg](/wiki/United_States_Army_Garrison_Heidelberg "United States Army Garrison Heidelberg")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Behoerdenzentrum_Heidelberg.jpg/250px-Behoerdenzentrum_Heidelberg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Behoerdenzentrum_Heidelberg.jpg)

Behördenzentrum Heidelberg

During World War II, Heidelberg was one of the few major cities in Germany not significantly damaged by Allied bombing. Situated in the [American Zone](/wiki/Allied-occupied_Germany#American_zone "Allied-occupied Germany") of Germany, Heidelberg became the headquarters of the American forces in Europe. The main military facilities were [Campbell Barracks](/wiki/Campbell_Barracks "Campbell Barracks"), the former Wehrmacht Grossdeutschland-Kaserne, which housed the US Army, Europe ([USAREUR](/wiki/USAREUR "USAREUR")) and [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO")'s Component Command-Land Headquarters. (Until 2004, this was designated Joint Headquarters Centre, and before that, LANDCENT).

Campbell Barracks and [Mark Twain Village](/wiki/Mark_Twain_Village "Mark Twain Village") were both in [Südstadt](/wiki/S%C3%BCdstadt,_Heidelberg "Südstadt, Heidelberg"); Patton Barracks was in nearby Kirchheim. Nachrichten Kaserne in [Rohrbach](/wiki/Rohrbach,_Heidelberg "Rohrbach, Heidelberg") was home to the former Heidelberg Army Hospital, later designated the Heidelberg Health Center. [Patrick Henry Village](/wiki/Patrick_Henry_Village "Patrick Henry Village"), the largest U.S. military housing area in the Heidelberg area, was west of Kirchheim. These installations, including Tompkins Barracks and Kilbourne Kaserne in nearby Schwetzingen, plus the Germersheim Depot, made up the U.S. Army Garrison Heidelberg. Tompkins Barracks was home to [U.S. Army Installation Management Command Europe Region](http://www.imcom-europe.army.mil). The Heidelberg U.S. Army Air Field (Heidelberg AAF) was converted to an heliport (mostly Blackhawk Helicopters) after the [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") [Kosovo](/wiki/Kosovo "Kosovo") campaign.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Heidelberg_Bahnstadt_Langer_Anger_2.JPG/250px-Heidelberg_Bahnstadt_Langer_Anger_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Heidelberg_Bahnstadt_Langer_Anger_2.JPG)

The New city district of Heidelberg, [Bahnstadt](/w/index.php?title=Bahnstadt&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bahnstadt (page does not exist)"), is one of the biggest [passive house](/wiki/Passive_house "Passive house") settlements in the world.

The children of [United States Department of Defense](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Defense "United States Department of Defense") employees based in Heidelberg attended on-base schools operated by the DODDS-E (Department of Defense Dependents Schools – Europe). There were three schools of this kind: [Heidelberg High School](/wiki/Heidelberg_High_School "Heidelberg High School") in Mark Twain Village (Mark Twain Elementary School closed at the completion of the 2010–2011 school year), and [Heidelberg Middle School](/wiki/Heidelberg_Middle_School "Heidelberg Middle School") and Patrick Henry Elementary in Patrick Henry Village.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

On 19 October 2009, the U.S. Army announced that it would be building new headquarters for USAREUR in [Wiesbaden](/wiki/Wiesbaden "Wiesbaden"). The move from Heidelberg took place in 2012 and 2013, and was completed in 2014.[[37]](#cite_note-37) By 2015 all United States forces had left Heidelberg. The barracks and the housing areas were returned to the German state for conversion to civilian use.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Culture")]

### Events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Events")]

Several festivals and events hosted and organized in Heidelberg throughout the year. In February, the *Ball der Vampire* (Ball of the Vampires)[[38]](#cite_note-38) is arranged and [Fasching](/wiki/Carnival_in_Germany,_Switzerland_and_Austria "Carnival in Germany, Switzerland and Austria"), the equivalent of Mardi Gras or Carnival in some German regions, with a giant vampire-themed costume party at the local castle or city hall is celebrated. In March or April the [Heidelberger Frühling](/wiki/Heidelberger_Fr%C3%BChling "Heidelberger Frühling"), the Classic Music Festival and the international Easter egg market are conducted. During the last weekend of April there is an annually organized half marathon. In the summertime there are the *Frühlingsmesse* on the Messeplatz (May) and Illumination of the castle and bridge with lights and fireworks take place. In September, on the last Saturday the *Old Town Autumn Festival* is held.[[39]](#cite_note-39) It includes a Medieval Market, an arts and crafts market, a flea market, and music from Samba to Rock. During October or November there are the *Heidelberger Theater Days* and the [Enjoy Jazz](/w/index.php?title=Enjoy_Jazz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Enjoy Jazz (page does not exist)") festival. Every year in November the [International Filmfestival Mannheim-Heidelberg](/wiki/International_Filmfestival_Mannheim-Heidelberg "International Filmfestival Mannheim-Heidelberg") take place in the city, too. The festival presents arthouse films of international newcomer directors and is held jointly by both of the cities.[[40]](#cite_note-40) During Christmas there is a Christmas market throughout the oldest part of the city. A famous gift is the chocolate called Heidelberger *Studentenkuss* (student kiss).

### Cinemas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Cinemas")]

The nationwide trend of cinema closures hit Heidelberg harder than many other places in Germany.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

Recent years saw the low-point of this development, when in this city of over 150,000 people there were just three small cinemas left, with a total capacity of under 450 seats.[[44]](#cite_note-44) The situation has slightly improved since late 2017, when Heidelberg got a new multiplex adjacent to the new Bahnstadt development, the "Luxor Filmpalast".

Luxor shows mainly [Blockbuster movies](/wiki/Blockbuster_(entertainment) "Blockbuster (entertainment)") in German, but some showings in English are on offer for selected films.[[45]](#cite_note-45)

The small 2-screen cinema "Gloria/Gloriette" (Old Town), together with the affiliated "Kamera" (Neuenheim) offers arthouse and independent films, with some showings being offered in the films original language, usually with German subtitles.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

The non-profit "Karlstorkino" (at the far eastern edge of the Old Town, next to the river) offers international arthouse fare and the occasional documentary. The vast majority of non-German films at this cinema are shown in their original language with either English or German subtitles, depending on the film.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

### Museums and exhibitions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Museums and exhibitions")]

Among the most prominent museums of Heidelberg are for instance the Carl Bosch Museum which shows life and work of chemist and Nobel Prize-winner [Carl Bosch](/wiki/Carl_Bosch "Carl Bosch"). Then there is the [Documentation and Culture Centre of German Sinti and Roma](/wiki/Documentation_and_Culture_Centre_of_German_Sinti_and_Roma "Documentation and Culture Centre of German Sinti and Roma") (Dokumentations- und Kulturzentrum Deutscher Sini und Roma) describing the Nazi genocide of the [Sinti](/wiki/Sinti "Sinti") and [Roma](/wiki/Romani_people "Romani people") peoples. The German Packing Museum (Deutsches Verpackungsmuseum) gives an overview of the history of packing and wrapping goods, whereas the German Pharmacy Museum (Deutsches Apothekenmuseum) which is located in the castle illustrates the history of Pharmacy in Germany. The *[Kurpfälzisches Museum](/wiki/Kurpf%C3%A4lzisches_Museum "Kurpfälzisches Museum")* (Palatinate Museum) offers a great art collection and some Roman archeological artifacts from the region. In honour of [Friedrich Ebert](/wiki/Friedrich_Ebert "Friedrich Ebert") the [President Friedrich Ebert Memorial](/wiki/President_Friedrich_Ebert_Memorial "President Friedrich Ebert Memorial"), which commemorates the life of Germany's first democratic head of state, was established. Besides, there are guided tours in most of the historical monuments of Heidelberg, as well as organized tourist tours through the city available in several languages.

### Heidelberg Romanticism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Heidelberg Romanticism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Carl_Philipp_Fohr_001.jpg/250px-Carl_Philipp_Fohr_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Carl_Philipp_Fohr_001.jpg)

Romantic view of [Heidelberg Castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_Castle "Heidelberg Castle") ruins by [Karl Philipp Fohr](/wiki/Karl_Philipp_Fohr "Karl Philipp Fohr"), 1815, Hessisches Landesmuseum Darmstadt

Heidelberg was the centre of the epoch of *Romantik* ([Romanticism](/wiki/German_Romanticism "German Romanticism")) in Germany. The phase after [Jena Romanticism](/wiki/Jena_Romanticism "Jena Romanticism") is often called **Heidelberg Romanticism** (see also [Berlin Romanticism](/wiki/Berlin_Romanticism "Berlin Romanticism")). There was a famous circle of poets (the **Heidelberg Romantics**), such as [Joseph von Eichendorff](/wiki/Joseph_von_Eichendorff "Joseph von Eichendorff"), [Johann Joseph von Görres](/wiki/Johann_Joseph_von_G%C3%B6rres "Johann Joseph von Görres"), [Ludwig Achim von Arnim](/wiki/Ludwig_Achim_von_Arnim "Ludwig Achim von Arnim"), and [Clemens Brentano](/wiki/Clemens_Brentano "Clemens Brentano"). A relic of Romanticism is the Philosophers' Walk (German: *Philosophenweg*), a scenic walking path on the nearby Heiligenberg, overlooking Heidelberg.

The *Romantik* epoch of German philosophy and literature, was described as a movement against classical and realistic theories of literature, a contrast to the rationality of the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"). It elevated [medievalism](/wiki/Medievalism "Medievalism") and elements of art and narrative perceived to be from the medieval period. It also emphasized folk art, nature, and an epistemology based on nature, which included human activity conditioned by nature in the form of language, custom and usage.

### Old Heidelberg

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Old Heidelberg")]

Further information: [Alt Heidelberg (play)](/wiki/Alt_Heidelberg_(play) "Alt Heidelberg (play)")

In 1901 [Wilhelm Meyer-Förster](/wiki/Wilhelm_Meyer-F%C3%B6rster "Wilhelm Meyer-Förster") wrote the play *[Old Heidelberg](/wiki/Old_Heidelberg_(play) "Old Heidelberg (play)")* which was followed by a large number of film adaptations. It was the basis for [Sigmund Romberg](/wiki/Sigmund_Romberg "Sigmund Romberg")'s 1924 operetta *[The Student Prince](/wiki/The_Student_Prince "The Student Prince")* which was itself turned into a [film of the same title](/wiki/The_Student_Prince_(film) "The Student Prince (film)").

### "I Lost My Heart in Heidelberg"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: \"I Lost My Heart in Heidelberg\"")]

Further information: [I Lost My Heart in Heidelberg (song)](/wiki/I_Lost_My_Heart_in_Heidelberg_(song) "I Lost My Heart in Heidelberg (song)")

The 1925 song "I Lost My Heart in Heidelberg" composed by [Fred Raymond](/wiki/Fred_Raymond "Fred Raymond") was a major hit and inspired a stage musical and two films. It remains the theme song of Heidelberg.

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Sport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/57/Cmglee_Heidelberg_Neckar_watersports.jpg/250px-Cmglee_Heidelberg_Neckar_watersports.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cmglee_Heidelberg_Neckar_watersports.jpg)

Kayaking, sailing and rowing near the Neckarinsel at sunset

Heidelberg is one of the centres of [Rugby union in Germany](/wiki/Rugby_union_in_Germany "Rugby union in Germany"), along with [Hanover](/wiki/Hanover "Hanover"). In [2008–09](/wiki/Rugby-Bundesliga_2008-09 "Rugby-Bundesliga 2008-09"), four out of nine clubs in the [Rugby-Bundesliga](/wiki/Rugby-Bundesliga "Rugby-Bundesliga") were from Heidelberg, these being [RG Heidelberg](/wiki/RG_Heidelberg "RG Heidelberg"), who play at the [Fritz-Grunebaum-Sportpark](/wiki/Fritz-Grunebaum-Sportpark "Fritz-Grunebaum-Sportpark"), [SC Neuenheim](/wiki/SC_Neuenheim "SC Neuenheim"), [Heidelberger RK](/wiki/Heidelberger_RK "Heidelberger RK"), and [TSV Handschuhsheim](/wiki/TSV_Handschuhsheim "TSV Handschuhsheim"). [Heidelberger TV](/wiki/Heidelberger_TV "Heidelberger TV") has a rugby department.

[Rugby League Deutschland](/wiki/Rugby_league_in_Germany "Rugby league in Germany") has two teams based in Heidelberg, Heidelberg Sharks formed in 2005 and Rohrbach Hornets formed in 2007.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/45/Academics_Heidelberg_huddle.jpg/250px-Academics_Heidelberg_huddle.jpg)](/wiki/File:Academics_Heidelberg_huddle.jpg)

Academics Heidelberg huddle in January 2023.

The city is also home to the [USC Heidelberg](/wiki/USC_Heidelberg "USC Heidelberg") (Academics Heidelberg), which won 9 [German Basketball Championships](/wiki/German_Basketball_Championship "German Basketball Championship") and remains the second most successful team in the history of German professional basketball. Today, the club plays in Germany's second division [ProA](/wiki/ProA "ProA"). It is primarily known for its youth department which developed several members of [Germany's senior national basketball team](/wiki/Germany%27s_senior_national_basketball_team "Germany's senior national basketball team").

[Heidelberger SC](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberger_SC&action=edit&redlink=1 "Heidelberger SC (page does not exist)") [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heidelberger_SC "de:Heidelberger SC")] and [SG Heidelberg-Kirchheim](/wiki/SG_Heidelberg-Kirchheim "SG Heidelberg-Kirchheim") are local [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") teams.

The city hosted events during the [1972 Summer Paralympics](/wiki/1972_Summer_Paralympics "1972 Summer Paralympics").

They also hosted the 2019 WU24 Championships from 13 to 20 July. It was the fifth edition of this world championship and marked the third time Germany has hosted an [ultimate frisbee](/wiki/Ultimate_frisbee "Ultimate frisbee") tournament.[[48]](#cite_note-48)

Germany's oldest tennis club, which was founded in 1890, is located in Heidelberg.

* Heidelberg also features during a mission in the [Electronic Arts](/wiki/Electronic_Arts "Electronic Arts") strategy game *[Red Alert 3](/wiki/Red_Alert_3 "Red Alert 3")*.
* *[Morris from America](/wiki/Morris_from_America "Morris from America")* takes places in Heidelberg.
* In the *[Watchmen](/wiki/Watchmen_(TV_series) "Watchmen (TV series)")* TV series which serves as alternate direct sequels to the original *Watchmen* graphic novel, Dr. Manhattan, aka Jonathan "Jon" Osterman, is said to be born in Heidelberg, Germany and immigrates to the US along with his father.
* Heidelberg is also revealed to be the home town of [Sergeant Schultz](/wiki/Sergeant_Schultz "Sergeant Schultz") on *[Hogan's Heroes](/wiki/Hogan%27s_Heroes "Hogan's Heroes")*.
* in a [Hallmark Channel](/wiki/Hallmark_Channel "Hallmark Channel") movie: *A Heidelberg Holiday*. (15 November 2023)
* In the sitcom *[The Big Bang Theory](/wiki/The_Big_Bang_Theory "The Big Bang Theory")*, [Sheldon Cooper](/wiki/Sheldon_Cooper "Sheldon Cooper") said he went to [Heidelberg University](/wiki/Heidelberg_University "Heidelberg University") as a visiting professor when he was a teenager. This is confirmed in the spin-off series *[Young Sheldon](/wiki/Young_Sheldon "Young Sheldon")*, where several episodes in Season 7 take place there.

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Literature")]

* [Heidelberg Castle](/wiki/Heidelberg_Castle "Heidelberg Castle") forms the setting for the beginning of [Mark Twain](/wiki/Mark_Twain "Mark Twain")'s story *[The Awful German Language](/wiki/The_Awful_German_Language "The Awful German Language")*.
* Most of [David Lodge](/wiki/David_Lodge_(author) "David Lodge (author)")'s novel *[Out of the Shelter](/wiki/Out_of_the_Shelter "Out of the Shelter")* takes place in Heidelberg in 1951 during the American occupation after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").
* Heidelberg is the home of a professional [Quidditch](/wiki/Quidditch "Quidditch") team operating within the fictional [Harry Potter universe](/wiki/Harry_Potter_universe "Harry Potter universe"): the [Heidelberg Harriers](/w/index.php?title=Heidelberg_Harriers&action=edit&redlink=1 "Heidelberg Harriers (page does not exist)") have been described as "fiercer than a dragon and twice as clever".[[50]](#cite_note-50)
* Heidelberg is the residence of Nina Fortner in [Naoki Urasawa](/wiki/Naoki_Urasawa "Naoki Urasawa")'s manga series *[Monster](/wiki/Monster_(manga) "Monster (manga)")*.
* Heidelberg also features in [Somerset Maugham](/wiki/Somerset_Maugham "Somerset Maugham")'s *[Of Human Bondage](/wiki/Of_Human_Bondage "Of Human Bondage")* and its film versions.
* Heidelberg also is he setting of some German crime novels, for example by [Wolfgang Burger](/w/index.php?title=Wolfgang_Burger&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wolfgang Burger (page does not exist)") (protagonist: Detective Gerlach) or [Carlo Schäfer](/wiki/Carlo_Sch%C3%A4fer "Carlo Schäfer") (protagonist: Detective Theurer).
* Band of Brothers — Stephen E. Ambrose’s Band of Brothers recounts Easy Company’s passage through Heidelberg; Ambrose records David K. Webster’s admiration for the city, describing its intact buildings, river promenade and collegiate atmosphere and calling Heidelberg, in effect, “paradise.” [[51]](#cite_note-51)
